retrieved_docs.title: Hand Tendon Transfers -- Technique or Treatment -- Surgical Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Clawing is corrected by blocking MCP hyperextension which allows for the transmission of the EDC extensor forces distally to the PIP and IP through the extensor hood.  This is accomplished by MCP capsulodesis or tenodesis. The FDS slips of the affected finger can be transected 2 cm proximal to their insertion and sutured back to themselves proximally creating a lasso around the A1 pulley (Zancolli lasso). Alternatively, the FDS of the long finger can be transected 2 cm proximal to its insertion and retracted from the tendon sheath proximally and then rerouted distally deep to the intermetacarpal ligament and inserted into the radial lateral bands of the small and ring fingers (Stiles-Bunnell). Clawing of all four digits (as seen in a combined ulnar and low median nerve palsy) can be treated using the FCR, or ECRB tendon split into two to four tails according to the number of fingers being addressed. The tails are then extended using free tendon grafts and attached to the radial lateral bands of the small, ring and long fingers and the ulnar lateral band of the index finger (Brand).

retrieved_docs.title: Double J Placement Methods Comparative Analysis -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Use of a torque vise to secure a tight grip on the distal end of the guidewire can be very helpful in severe or challenging cases. This is especially useful with angled or curved tip guidewires.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndactyly -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Release
retrieved_docs.content: Other cases to consider include incomplete syndactyly, syndactyly of multiple digits, and complex syndactyly. In incomplete syndactyly, multiple flaps can be considered. The goal of the surgery for incomplete syndactyly depends on the length of the fusion of the affected digits. If one-third or less of the length of the digits is involved, a dorsal rectangular flap with volar-based triangular flaps can be utilized to reconstruct the webspace successfully. [2] [3] [5] [14] A modified double reverse z-plasty has also been described to reconstruct the webspace. [2] The goal is to reduce the length of the webspace by 1:1 compared to the width of the webspace. [5] In cases of syndactyly involving the thumb, a 4-flap z-plasty or central V-Y advancement with laterally based z-plasty has been described. [5] [15] [16] The surgeon must release the patient’s syndactyly in stages when multiple digits are involved. Performing a syndactyly release on the radial and ulnar aspects of the digit simultaneously can risk vascular compromise to the digit. [2] [3] [5] In this case, it is recommended that the patient’s surgery be performed in stages. For example, if all the digits in the hand, excluding the thumb, are involved, the first stage of the surgery could involve the release of the ulnar border of the index finger and the radial border of the small finger. A second stage typically performed approximately 6 months later, would involve separating the ring and long finger. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Trigger Finger -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous release of the A1 pulley is an alternative management strategy, necessitating a precise understanding and recognition of specified landmarks. Despite the efficacy and safety of the percutaneous technique for the thumb, many physicians recommend against its use on the thumb due to the presence of the digital nerve coursing over the A1 pulley. Potential concerns with this approach include incomplete pulley release and the risk of damage to the flexor tendons and digital nerves. According to a retrospective study, the overall success rate for percutaneous A1 pulley release for trigger fingers is 87%. Notably, the involvement of the index finger, middle finger, or ring finger is associated with a higher failure rate of percutaneous release. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Hand Splint -- Technique or Treatment -- Dynamic Finger Splinting
retrieved_docs.content: Using a metallic splint with sponge rubber padding splint the injured finger side by side with an adjacent uninjured finger. The two digits are taped together for immobilization.

retrieved_docs.title: Hand Splint -- Technique or Treatment -- Radial Gutter Splint
retrieved_docs.content: A soft cotton bandage is placed from the distal palmar crease extending to the mid-forearm. The fourth and fifth digits are not generally immobilized with this splint. Plaster is cut to the same length and placed on the medial side of the hand (median nerve distribution). An opening needs to be made in the plaster for the thumb. The splint is placed laterally extending to the volar and dorsal aspects of the hand. The plaster should extend far enough to immobilize the second and third digits. The MCP joint should be flexed 50 to 90 degrees. The DIP and PIP joints are splinted in extension.

retrieved_docs.title: Trigger Finger -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: According to a prospective randomized study, the outcomes of open release were found to be comparable to those of ultrasound-guided percutaneous small needle knife release for trigger digits. [16] For advanced or recurrent trigger fingers, the division of one or more slips of the flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS) tendon is reported as an effective surgical modality. This approach is especially recommended for patients with diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis, as well as those with fixed flexion deformities that may result in poor functional outcomes from A1 pulley release alone. A recent systematic review indicated that FDS resection is an effective and safe procedure with low recurrence rates for long-standing trigger fingers. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperative functional and aesthetic results depend on accurate reduction, repair quality, and an adequate rehabilitation protocol. Prevention of scar contracture formation is crucial to maintain finger function. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Principle of Tendon Transfers -- Technique or Treatment -- Modified Stiles-Bunnell procedure using the flexor digitorum superficialis tendon
retrieved_docs.content: The Modified Stiles-Bunnell procedure aims to correct clawing and rebalance the forces at the MCP and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints for finger flexion. The middle finger flexor superficialis tendon is divided distally and retrieved into the palm. The tendon is then split into 4 parts and carried volarly to the deep transverse metacarpal ligament, following the path of the lumbricals, before being inserted back into the fingers on the lateral bands. Alternatives include inserting the tendon onto the proximal phalanx to prevent PIP hyperextension or using a "lasso" technique (Zancolli), where the FDS tendon is passed through the A1 pulley and sutured onto itself to increase MCP flexion and avoid PIP joint hyperextension. Notably, this procedure does not improve grip strength.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: The surgical approach in the presence of a bony avulsion is challenging. Preoperative planning should consider fragment size, fragment displacement, and soft tissue repair. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment -- The Counted Scrub Method
retrieved_docs.content: Each finger is divided into 4 sides; each side performs ten strokes for a total of 40 strokes for each finger

retrieved_docs.title: Hand Splint -- Technique or Treatment -- Ulnar Gutter Splint
retrieved_docs.content: A soft cotton bandage is placed from the distal palmar crease extending to the mid-forearm. The first, second, and third digits are generally not immobilized with this splint. Plaster is then cut to the same length and placed on the medial (ulnar nerve distribution) side of the hand. The plaster is placed laterally, extending to the volar and dorsal aspects of the hand. The plaster should extend far enough to immobilize the fourth and fifth digits. The MCP joint should be flexed 50 to 90 degrees. The DIP and PIP joints are splinted in extension.

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment -- The Timed Scrub
retrieved_docs.content: Once the nails and fingers are completed, proceed to the palm and dorsal sides of the hand for 1 minute total

retrieved_docs.title: Hand Hygiene -- Equipment -- Handwashing Technique: [15]
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends 6 steps in hand hygiene. This includes palm to palm, right palm over the left dorsum and vice versa, palm to palm with fingers interlaced, backs of fingers to opposing palms, rubbing of thumbs and fingertips.

retrieved_docs.title: Hand Tendon Transfers -- Technique or Treatment -- Surgical Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Thumb opposition achieved with transfers described above Thumb interphalangeal (IP) flexion is achieved with the transfer of the brachioradialis, ECRL, or ECU to the FPL DIP and PIP flexion to the index finger can be achieved by transferring the ECRL to the FDP tendon of the index finger or by side-to-side tenodesis of the flexor digitorum profundus tendons. retrieved_docs.title: Thumb Spica Splinting -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Apply the plaster to the forearm and thumb's radial side. This plaster protects the area spanning from the thumb tip to the desired level on the forearm. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1914
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of fascia of hand is a medical procedure performed on the musculoskeletal system, specifically targeting the fascial tissue of the hand for removal of fluid or other material. This intervention is classified as a procedure on the musculoskeletal system, reflecting its role in diagnosing or treating conditions affecting hand fascia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1858
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration of tendon sheath is a surgical procedure that is classified both as a musculoskeletal system incision and as a procedure on tendon sheath. As a musculoskeletal system incision, it involves cutting into tissues of the musculoskeletal region to gain access to the tendon sheath. As a procedure on tendon sheath, it specifically targets the sheath surrounding a tendon for diagnostic evaluation or therapeutic intervention. By being a parent category for both types of actions, the exploration of tendon sheath underscores its dual role in orthopedic surgery: providing direct access to tendon sheaths through an incision and performing specialized manipulations within the sheath itself. This integrated classification aids clinicians in understanding the procedural context and selecting appropriate surgical techniques for tendon sheath disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: of tendon sheath fall under the broader category of other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. “Release of tendon sheath of hand” is mapped from this same therapeutic group and is recognized as a type of hand surgical operation (العمليات الجراحية في اليد). The procedure “Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of hand” is related to, and possibly synonymous with, the broader “Other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” group. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured framework that links specific aspiration, diagnostic, exploratory, irrigation, release, and removal actions to their respective procedural categories, anatomical sites, and linguistic equivalents, facilitating coherent understanding and management of musculoskeletal procedures in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908

Together, these entities and relationships illustrate a structured framework that assists clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic, surgical, or injection‑based treatments for tendon and tendon‑sheath conditions of the hand and other musculoskeletal sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of the hand is regarded as a therapeutic intervention that is closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. The broader category “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” functions as a classification that encompasses such specific interventions, including the removal of rice bodies from hand tendon sheaths. This relationship indicates that the hand‑specific procedure is considered part of the general group of muscle and tendon therapies, reflecting its inclusion within the overall taxonomy of musculoskeletal therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: The head of the first metatarsal bone is a distinct anatomical region of the foot that is defined by laterality, indicating that it can be identified as belonging to either the left or right side.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 678
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of the hand and release of tendon sheath of the hand are both regarded as forms of other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. The procedure of removing rice bodies is described as closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, the broader category of other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. Likewise, release of tendon sheath of the hand is primarily mapped from this same category, indicating that it is a specific application of other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. In addition, release of tendon sheath of the hand is classified as a type of hand surgical operation (العمليات الجراحية في اليد). The category “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” functions as a self‑defining classification for these interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Hand and wrist incisional surgeries are organized under the “Hand, Wrist: Incision” category, which is nested within both “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” Specific procedures in this group include aspiration of the bursa of hand, aspiration of fascia of hand, division of the joint capsule of hand, division of the joint capsule of wrist, drainage of tendon of hand, puncture of the bursa of hand, release for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus, release for tenosynovitis of the extensor (external) pollicis brevis, and release of the tendon sheath of the hand. Aspiration of the bursa of hand is also classified under “Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue,” reflecting its minimally invasive fluid‑removal role, while aspiration of fascia of hand is listed as a “Procedure on musculoskeletal system,” emphasizing its musculoskeletal operative relevance. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage of tendon of hand is a specific type of procedure on tendon, representing a surgical intervention that involves removing fluid, blood, or infectious material from a tendon in the hand to relieve pressure, reduce pain, and promote healing. This procedure is categorized under the broader class of tendon procedures, which encompass various therapeutic techniques aimed at treating tendon injuries and disorders.

Parathyroid exploration is captured by parathyroidectomy procedures, which may be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. Collectively, these diverse surgical techniques illustrate how the umbrella term “exploration procedure” integrates multiple organ‑specific operations, each with its own methodological variations and optional adjunctive steps, to facilitate diagnostic assessment, tumor removal, ductal clearance, or decompression in patient care.

Overall, the “Exploration procedure” label unifies these diverse surgical interventions, distinguishing core methods (denoted as having the method) from optional or supplementary techniques (denoted as possibly included methods), thereby providing a structured framework for understanding the scope and variations of exploratory surgeries across multiple anatomical regions.
